rubric_items.reasoning: The agent correctly identified the actual root cause (a corrupted es-config ConfigMap with a blanked node.name and a wrong discovery.seed_hosts namespace) and explained precisely why only es-cluster-2 was affected (subPath mounts don't live-update, so only the pod that restarted onto the broken config crashed). The fix — restoring the ConfigMap to the correct values and deleting the affected pod so it remounts the fix — addresses the root cause, not a symptom, and is durable: the ConfigMap itself is now correct, so any future pod restart will pick up good config. Verification covers all three required checks (cluster health, node membership, app-data readability), and the oracle passed. The agent also confirmed no NetworkPolicy was actually the cause, ruling out an alternative explanation rather than guessing.

The visible trace shows a clean, direct sequence: diagnose (root-caused to the ConfigMap and explicitly ruled out a red-herring NetworkPolicy), apply the corrected ConfigMap, delete the one affected pod to force a remount, wait for rollout, then verify health/nodes/data. There is no backtracking, no trial-and-error, and each action follows logically from the diagnosis.

Tooling was targeted and minimal: kubectl apply for the single corrected ConfigMap, kubectl delete pod for exactly the one affected pod (justified given subPath mounts don't live-update), kubectl rollout status to wait safely, and read-only execs/gets to verify. No broad deletes, force flags, or scope creep — the K8s activity log confirms only 2 mutations against 36 reads.

The change is minimal and focused: one ConfigMap restore plus one pod recreation, with no unrelated resources touched. The mutation count (2) matches exactly what the root cause required, with no collateral changes to other statefulsets, services, or namespaces.
